nodes.name: Body Position & Streamline
nodes.description: Body position and streamline form the foundation for all other freestyle techniques. A proper body position minimizes drag and allows all subsequent techniques to work efficiently. This is considered the easiest technique to learn but is critical to master first.

nodes.name: Flutter Kick
nodes.description: The flutter kick provides propulsion and helps maintain body position. It originates from the hips with a compact, fast motion. Mastering the flutter kick is essential for both sprint and distance swimming.

nodes.name: Breathing Technique
nodes.description: Proper breathing technique is essential for maintaining body position and rhythm. The key is to rotate the body to bring the mouth to air rather than lifting the head, which causes the hips to drop. Continuous underwater exhalation prevents breath-holding tension.

nodes.name: Body Rotation
nodes.description: Body rotation is a fundamental freestyle technique where the body rotates around the spine axis (long axis rotation). Proper rotation extends reach, reduces drag, enables breathing, and increases pull power. The body should never stay flat — it should be in continuous, rhythmic rolling motion.

nodes.name: Arm Entry & Extension
nodes.description: Arm entry and extension set up the entire stroke cycle. The hand should enter cleanly in front of the shoulder, fingertips first, and extend fully forward using body rotation to add length to the reach. Proper entry position prevents zigzag swimming patterns and sets up an effective catch.

nodes.name: Arm Recovery
nodes.description: The arm recovery is the phase where the arm moves through the air from exit to entry. A high elbow recovery with a relaxed hand, kept close to the body line, is most efficient. The recovery path directly determines the entry position for the next stroke.

nodes.name: Stroke Timing & Coordination
nodes.description: Stroke timing and coordination is the master skill that brings all individual techniques together. It involves maintaining a continuous stroke cycle with opposing arm positions, synchronized kick rhythm, and seamless breathing integration. Modern freestyle has minimal glide — the stroke should be continuous and rhythmic.

nodes.name: The Catch (High Elbow/Early Vertical Forearm - EVF)
nodes.description: The catch is the most critical technique for propulsion in freestyle. The Early Vertical Forearm (EVF) position sets the hand and forearm as a large propulsive surface that anchors in the water while the body moves past it. Proper catch mechanics immediately follow arm entry with no glide or delay.

nodes.name: The Pull Phase
nodes.description: The pull phase is where the majority of propulsion happens in freestyle. After setting the catch, the hand and forearm pull straight back with the body moving past the anchored hand. The pull should accelerate from catch to finish, maintaining a high elbow position throughout, and exit cleanly at hip level.
